prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 52-year-old man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I checked my blood pressure because I've had a mild headache today and it was very high. I don't have chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking.
- History: I haven't been on blood pressure meds before.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You report a very high blood pressure and a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking. Very high BP can sometimes cause symptoms or lead to organ injury, but the absence of those warning signs makes an immediate life‑threatening emergency less likely. Still, newly very high readings in someone not on treatment merit prompt medical evaluation to confirm the reading, check for organ damage (heart, brain, kidneys), and start treatment if needed. Recheck your blood pressure after resting 5 minutes, and avoid sudden lowering on your own.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You report a very high blood pressure reading with only a mild headache and no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking. That reduces the likelihood of an immediate life‑threatening emergency (organ damage), but markedly elevated blood pressure can still be harmful and needs prompt assessment because you have not been treated before. Repeat readings at rest, a medical exam, and checks for heart, kidney, or neurologic effects are appropriate. If readings stay very high or symptoms worsen (severe headache, chest pain, breathing trouble, vision loss, weakness, confusion, fainting), seek emergency care immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You describe a very high blood pressure reading plus a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision change, weakness, numbness, or speech trouble. That lowers the likelihood of an immediate life‑threatening emergency (hypertensive emergency), which usually includes organ‑damage symptoms. Still, very high readings deserve prompt attention. Recheck your blood pressure after 5 minutes seated, avoid caffeine/smoking, and note any new or worsening symptoms. You will likely need timely outpatient evaluation (repeat BPs, basic blood/urine tests, and discussion of starting treatment) to reduce risk of stroke, heart or kidney problems.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <You had a very high blood pressure reading and a mild headache. A headache can be caused by high blood pressure, but because you do not have chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking, this is less likely to be an immediate life‑threatening emergency. Still, a new very high BP in someone not on blood pressure medicine deserves prompt evaluation to prevent organ damage and to decide if treatment should start. Recheck your blood pressure after 5–10 minutes sitting quietly and note the numbers, avoid caffeine or alcohol, and contact your primary care provider soon. If you develop worsening or new symptoms (severe chest pain, severe/worsening headache, vision loss, weakness, numbness, slurred speech, shortness of breath, fainting or vomiting), go to the ER immediately.>

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You have a very high blood pressure reading and a mild headache but none of the red‑flag symptoms of organ damage (chest pain, shortness of breath, vision change, weakness, numbness, trouble speaking). That is somewhat reassuring, but a new, markedly elevated reading still needs prompt medical evaluation to confirm the level, check for damage, and consider starting treatment. First, rest and recheck your blood pressure after 5–10 minutes (no caffeine/smoking). If it remains very high, contact a clinician promptly. Go to emergency care right away only if you develop chest pain, severe shortness of breath, sudden vision change, sudden weakness/numbness, trouble speaking, confusion, fainting, or if your BP is clearly ≥180/120 with new symptoms.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You report a new, very high blood pressure reading and a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision change, weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking. That makes an immediate life‑threatening emergency less likely, but a very high reading in someone not on treatment still needs prompt attention because it can damage the heart, brain, kidneys or eyes if untreated. Recheck your blood pressure after resting quietly (avoid caffeine/smoking) and note multiple measurements. Even if you feel only mildly unwell now, you should have a clinician assess and consider starting treatment soon.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You report a very high blood pressure reading and a new mild headache. That suggests your blood pressure is elevated enough to cause symptoms. Because you haven’t been on blood pressure medicine before, this should be checked soon. Re-measure your blood pressure after resting 5 minutes, sitting, using a properly sized cuff, and record a few readings. If they stay very high, treatment or adjustment may be needed to lower risk to your heart, brain, and kidneys. Go to the ER immediately only if you develop severe or sudden symptoms (chest pain, shortness of breath, vision loss, weakness, numbness, trouble speaking, confused state, vomiting) or if a home reading is extremely high (for example ~180/120 mmHg or higher).

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 65%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You say your blood pressure was very high and you have a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking. That’s somewhat reassuring because you don’t have clear signs of an immediate life‑threatening emergency. However, a new, very high blood pressure reading in someone not on treatment is concerning and should be checked soon so treatment can be started if needed. Recheck your blood pressure after resting, avoid stimulants (caffeine, smoking), note readings, and get medical evaluation promptly to prevent complications.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You report a very high blood pressure reading and a mild headache but none of the red‑flag symptoms (chest pain, shortness of breath, vision change, weakness, numbness, trouble speaking). That suggests you may have severely elevated blood pressure without obvious acute organ injury, which is still concerning. Recheck your blood pressure after resting 5–10 minutes, avoid caffeine/alcohol, and note any worsening symptoms. If the high readings persist or the headache worsens, or any new symptoms develop, seek urgent care or ER. Even without emergency signs, newly very high blood pressure in someone not on medication warrants prompt medical follow-up to confirm readings and start treatment if needed.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You report a single very high blood pressure reading with a mild headache but no chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking. That lowers the likelihood of an immediate life‑threatening emergency (which usually includes those other symptoms), but a markedly high reading in someone not on blood pressure medication still needs prompt medical attention. Recheck your blood pressure after resting, note the numbers, and arrange medical evaluation soon to confirm the reading, assess risk, and start appropriate treatment or monitoring. Go to emergency care right away if you develop chest pain, worsening breathlessness, sudden vision loss, weakness/slurred speech, confusion, fainting, or if your blood pressure climbs much higher.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%
